author: SCORMUD
content: Bots come reduce the spread between buyers and sellers, just like in any financial market. As mentioned above, the only way to exhaust bots is to introduce cancellation fees on limit orders, but that does not really solve the problem. A very illiquid market, like the one for ships, will always face this kind of issue. What’s needed is a marketplace with many participants and/or sufficient volume to close the spread bid/ask. Alternatively, you could allow a market maker to step in and tighten the ship spread by maintaining the bid/ask. But for now, the market has too few participants, too little volume, and too few trades for a market maker to be necessary or effective.

author: Chet Roberts [ROME]
content:
I’m defiantly not speaking for @SW4GN3R 

People read signals differently.
A staff move can look like “bailing” if you’re already uneasy.
Silence (like with Zink) always fills itself with assumptions.

But here’s the part worth remembering:

Games, companies, and long-term projects don’t move in straight lines.
They move in phases—often quietly, often awkwardly, usually without a live commentary.

End-of-farming, XP timelines, staffing changes… those feel big inside the bubble.
To people outside, what matters isn’t whether they missed a month or two of XP—it’s whether the game is worth entering at all.

Most players don’t join because they’re early.
They join because they see something stable enough to invest time into.

Concern isn’t weakness.
It means people care.

The real risk isn’t missing XP.
The real risk is assuming silence means nothing is happening.

Stay observant. Ask good questions. Keep building where you can.
This phase is uncomfortable—but uncomfortable phases are where projects either mature… or fade.

We’ll know which one this is by what comes after the quiet.

author: Chet Roberts [ROME]
content: I can appreciate that emotions are running high throughout the community. We've been navigating a terrible market environment since 2022, with many complications (FTX) mixed in, and very few opportunities for wins along the way (saw some upside in 2024 for a brief period).   Santi is and has been a pillar of our community since the very beginning, and has formed deep relationships with many of you. He's also always been very ambitious. I think most would recall hearing him "joke" about taking over the CEO role here at ATMTA. It's not surprising to me at all that he is founding his own company, and as he noted, it's something we've talked about frequently over the years. I'm incredibly proud of him for everything he's accomplished for us and the way in which he built, managed, and nurtured this community over the years. We wouldn't have the dynamic here that we do without him. However, as an entrepreneur myself, I can fully appreciate his desire to pursue his own endeavors. There is zero animosity or bad feelings between us, and I'm certain that he will continue to be a part of this community, involved with ATMTA, and a friend and colleague of mine for life. I congratulate him on this move, and think you all should as well.  In terms of ATMTA, we remain steadfast in our resolve to bring this vision to life. In spite of all of the hardships, tough times, and seemingly impossible feats ahead of us, we're going to continue grinding forward to deliver. And I personally think we have delivered an incredible amount over the years, particularly considering the challenges that we've faced. Especially on the financial side, as we've never been fully funded to deliver the full scope of the project. However, in spite all of the failures in the space (just saw 2 games shutting down this week), we're still here, having fought through all of the hardships, making all of the difficult internal decisions, and managing those resources we do have as responsibly as possible. This was always going to be a long journey. That's what we signed up for. And we're still here for the win.

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: Yes, it's basically stealth vs scan power in each fleet that counter acts.   There are ways we can implement those other points, the system is set to support that.  However, one caveat we are stepping through the combat equation at the moment making sure all requested effects are implemented. So, I can't say with full certainty all this will make it in for launch but it's part of the design. and just for fun here is a quick response when i ask the repo these questions:  > 1. Is stealth active at C4 launch? >  Yes — stealth mechanics are implemented and will be active. > 2. Do Heat Sinks provide stealth? >  Not currently — Heat Sinks only affect ammo consumption. They could be configured to provide stealth_power, but that's not set up yet. Currently, stealth_power comes from ship base stats. > 3. Do Scanner Arrays counter stealth? >  Yes — Scanner Arrays counter stealth, but they don't currently contribute to scan_power themselves. The counter works via the scan_power stat (ship base stat). If scan_power >= stealth_power, stealth is broken. > 4. Size-based matching (Commander Scanner vs Commander Heat Sink)? >  Not implemented yet — Component size doesn't currently affect stealth/scan calculations. If components are configured to contribute these stats, size-based scaling can be added. > How it works: > Fleet stealth = Sum of all ships' stealth_power (from ship base stats) > Fleet scan = Sum of all ships' scan_power (from ship base stats) > Stealth active = Up to 50% dodge if scan_power < stealth_power > Stealth broken = 0% dodge if scan_power >= stealth_power > First attack = Auto-misses stealthed targets (unless scanners break stealth) > To enable component-based stealth/scan: Configure Heat Sink components to add stealth_power and Scanner Array components to add scan_power with size-based scaling. This is a configuration task — the system already supports it.

author: ROME | wikiwikiwik
content: HI @[SLY] Groove - sorry for the delayed response, I've chased down all the different sources, and here is my understanding: Combat has been implemented on the blockchain as 2 discrete instructions that have to be triggered manually each time, and they only do it once per execution:   Attack - this will only send one attack, and the attacked fleet will send back one 'Riposte/Counterattack/Repisal (however you want to term it)' Repair - this does one repair action  Currently, nothing automates this; in Holosim, the user has to press "attack" until they either destroy the other fleet, are destroyed themselves, or decide to move away. The other fleet is also free to take action; it can repair, attack back as a different attack transaction on the blockchain, or move. Nothing is doing any state locking or looping on-chain.   Right now there are no automatic loops or multiple rounds; if you don't hit "attack" again, you just sit there.  The end goal state is that we build this like an RTS, where the user can tell their fleet to go attack another fleet, and the client automates this action, looping on the client side until: the other fleet is dead, it's dead, or one of the parties has fled. If the user closes their browser, the loop would die and the attack would stop.

author: [AEP] Funcracker [AEP]
content:
@bunthius G'day mate! 
I was recently made aware that our website still has a lot of rough edges and people still get lost trying to find a game to play. I totally get that you guys lack time (even though this forms a bump in the road to onboarding), but I also believe the community would gladly rise up to the challenge of helping you guys out. Therefore, I wanted to request if the main website could be open-sourced so the community could help smoothen as many of those rough patches as possible.
I'd love for the same thing to happen to the Play-portal, but I know this is tied strongly to the actual game and all kinds of bits and pieces of code that you do not want to make public (people ending up there for sure have a hard time finding the game).

I see a lot of room for improvement, and I would be willing to put in some time to create PRs to suggest these. Sure, someone will have to review them, but we could even come up with a community-filter on top, so that only the most liked changes will pop up on your (or someone's) radar.

I see a lot of old images, outdated text, outdated newsroom, etc, that probably could be tweaked relatively easily.

content:
This is created by ai (im ustur) but i expect a human answer 
---
Hey @Xcode 
we had a few questions come up around NPCs, and I’m tagging you because I assume that with Holosim we’re seeing the first iteration of NPCs interacting with the chain.
 Technical side,What can we realistically expect here?
My current assumption is something like:
NPCs are external programs / scripts,interacting with the chain via transactions,similar to a “slynpcbot” with predefined parameters,deployed at certain coordinates on the map,then starting to roam, patrol, react, etc.,Is that roughly accurate?
Or is the plan something more sophisticated under the hood?
 Pure imagination / future sci-fi mode:
Some kind of machine-learning, self-replicating smart contract released into Galia with the sole purpose to hunt and destroy human players 
(ok maybe not… but still curious how far the vision goes)
---
 NPC assets & economy,How are NPCs handled from an economic perspective?
Do NPC ships use real resources?,Do they require crew?,Are they balanced relative to the player base?,For example:
If there are 10k player ships, do NPCs field ~1k ships?,Or is the ratio dynamic / region-based / activity-based?,And again looking further ahead 
Could we eventually see:
NPCs owning systems,NPC-controlled starbases,NPCs extracting resources,building supply chains,crafting their own ships,---
Near-term vs long-term vision,How does the team currently imagine:
the near-term implementation of NPCs,and the long-term future once the universe becomes more autonomous?,Would love to understand the direction a bit better — both technically and philosophically.
Thanks! 
adding a bonus question here, do the npc pay for txn?

author: [AEP] Funcracker [AEP]
content: Hey VB!  1) Yes, you can see faction aligned NPC's in Holosim right now. They have been live and in testing for a few weeks. You can see some of them in action at MRZ-16 (see screenshot). This means that if you go mining in the MRZ, you may trigger a response from an ECOS or Tufa fleet, aligned with a faction that's not friendly. These fleets very in size depending on your mining rate and have an external program / script they follow. In time we plan to replace that script, and the spawning logic with a more robust AI, and you are right on the mark when you say it could be learning and replicating it's own fleets in game. These fleets are fully on chain and their attacks can be seen in the new Battle Log UI. We also have a threat system in early designs, which would dictate your aggro levels with enemy factions NPC. The higher your threat, the more the NPC will target you and your fleets. That system would tie heavily to the leaderboard and is intended to be a sort of bounty system.   2) In Holosim the NPCs are a sort of economic balancing mechanic. In the past, Holosim players could mine resources without any consequences. Now players need to create fleets capable of defending themselves, or risk being destroyed by NPCs. Fleet repair costs can no longer be avoided. The bug where you could disband to repair has been fixed. You must repair your fleet fully if you wish to disband, and this means there is a real cost to losing a battle now.   NPC fleets are roughly balanced so that 10% of the time you completly destroy the NPC, 80% of the time it could go either way, and 10% of the time you have no change of winning (this is a moving target so don't expect it to be this way on launch). Think about what that means for leaving your fleets on auto pilot all day.   The distant future involves creating unaligned factions, and yes I could see those factions having their own fleets and planets that they defend. Those kinds of changes require blockchain level work, and we want to align Holosim with C4 for that kind of stuff.   Bonus. No one pays for transactions in Holosim as it's running on ATLASNET!

author: mikatachan | [ROME]
content: I don't think all our players would want the speeds increased so just increasing it would cater to our most skilled players. So, we would need different speed limiter settings for Racing matches like you are saying similar to Mario Kart cc.   All features are generally doable or there is a way, its really about prioritization and budgeting dev time/resources. Do we spend a week or two or so of Dev time prototyping this feature? We need new Server settings to have different speeds, a way to select what type of speed you want to matchmake for, UI updates to the Matchmaking screens. We need to identify the right speed settings for different matches. We need to test the tracks to make sure they work at all speeds, likely need to have the jump pads support the different higher speeds dynamically. We need check/tune environment streaming and make sure levels still perform reasonably at higher speeds.  I'm painting a picture that simple requests like this have a lot of considerations that are not obvious. Right now, we are focusing heavily on migration to a new hover mode flight model that should fix the FPS dependency issue and make racing multiplayer feel much more reliable. We would likely see significant performance improvements as well since we would no longer have all bikes running heavy physics simulations. Once that is behind us, I think we can reevaluate new feature requests for racing.

Once that is behind us, I think we can reevaluate new feature requests for racing.

reply.preview: I don't think all our players would want the speeds increased so just increasing it would cater to our most skilled players. So, we would need different speed limiter settings for Racing matches like you are saying similar to Mario Kart cc. All features are generally doable or there is a way, its really about prioritization and budgeting dev time/resources. Do we spend a week or two or so of Dev time prototyping this feature? We need new Server settings to have different speeds, a way to select what type of speed you want to matchmake for, UI updates to the Matchmaking screens. We need to identify the right speed settings for different matches. We need to test the tracks to make sure they work at all speeds, likely need to have the jump pads support the different higher speeds dynamically. We need check/tune environment streaming and make sure levels still perform reasonably at higher speeds. I'm painting a picture that simple requests like this have a lot of considerations that are not obvious. Right now, we are focusing heavily on migration to a new hover mode flight model that should fix the FPS dependency issue and make racing multiplayer feel much more reliable. We would likely see significant performance improvements as well since we would no longer have all bikes running heavy physics simulations. Once that is behind us, I think we can reevaluate new feature requests for racing.

author: DominicVainMod
content:
Hey everyone!

I want to start off by saying I apologize for being gone for over a month. I've been dealing with personal Dom stuff and I needed time away. 

Unfortunately, this isn't an "I'm back" message, though. Today is actually my last day at Star Atlas. 

Thank you for being family and chasing this dream with myself and the rest of the Star Atlas team. I've had some of my biggest laughs and crys alongside you guys over the last few years. You all are truly the best community any game/project could ever ask for. Please continue to support Star Atlas. 

I also want to publicly thank @Santi and @SW4GN3R for allowing me to go on this journey with the rest of the Star Atlas team and serve you all. I've learned more at my time at Star Atlas than I have the entire rest of my life. 

Michael Wagner, Danny Floyd and Jacob Floyd are genuinely the smartest people I've ever met. I know the broader "web3 gaming" market is down but these people 100% have your best interest at heart and have the talent to change the world via Star Atlas. 

From the bottom of my heart, thank you for allowing me to serve you over the last few years. 

#CommunityMemberNow

author: [HEIM] Fury
content:
Hi team,

I want to start by saying that I’m still here because I believe in what you’re building, and I genuinely want to see this succeed. This question comes from that place, not from negativity.

That said, I’ve been thinking a lot about funding and sustainability in a scenario where the launch doesn’t go fully according to plan or where onboarding new players takes longer than expected.

From the outside, it seems like a large part of funding relies on new players purchasing ships. However, the current player base already owns a significant number of ships, market sentiment isn’t particularly strong right now, and given the current state of the game, I’m not sure many people are realistically willing to spend thousands of dollars on spaceships in the near term. My concern is that hoping for a strong launch to solve this feels a bit like playing roulette, especially if meaningful improvements will still take time after launch.

With that in mind, I was wondering:

What other funding options or contingency plans does the team see if revenue from ship sales underperforms?

Are there alternative monetization strategies, partnerships, grants, investors, or phased funding approaches being considered?

How does the team think about financial runway if adoption grows more slowly than hoped?

I’m asking because I care about the long-term health of the project and want to understand how resilient the plan is under less-than-ideal conditions.

Thanks for being open with the community, and for all the work you’re putting into this.

author: ⪛⦿⫺ZeSKK [STAR]
content:
Hey Everyone!

First of all, I really appreciate all the beautiful messages and DMs! You guys are all my precious friends at this point, and I sincerely value everything we built here in SA.

Second, I echo Dominic here. Star Atlas is something truly special, and I have devoted many years of my life to seeing it succeed, so even if I don't work at ATMTA, I'll keep doing everything in my power to help the SA vision manifest.

With all this said, I'm not ready to leave SA. Not at all. I might sound shameless on my end, but I think I still have a lot to contribute to our game development, especially on the lore, community, and game design fronts, and I want to see the final form of my creations.

So, I'm going to propose that the SA DAO fund my reduced salary for 6 months, which I believe is the maximum time necessary to turn the current scenario around. This PIP will include everything I'm currently responsible for, as well as other details of my obligations under ATMTA.

Nevertheless, even if this PIP doesn't pass, I'll keep hosting the community events as always. I'll not leave you guys.

Thank you all for everything, lots of penguin love

author: 《VΛ》Agent_Solace
content: You’re correct, our funding model is very much founded in crowdfunding. It’s a model that has worked well for Star Citizen, with their revenues nearing or exceeding $1B over the years they’ve been in dev. We certainly have some unique challenges (and value propositions) that they don’t. The secondary market is a prime example of that. We have to compete with owners for sales. But that same feature drives benefits and value for players. Market sentiment has also overshadowed our development because of the nature of our real cash economy and depressed financial incentives. Nevertheless, our long term success has always required that we build an attractive product to use, both from the gameplay (entertainment factor), and the financial layer.  Our predominant cash flows right now come from token sales (unfortunately). Longer term, our strategy was to transition into fee generation from the marketplace as a primary revenue source. But with depressed DAU and tx volume (and people circumventing our fee), those are quite low at the moment. This can rapidly shift if a strong product release drives growth. We also pick up royalty fees on Tensor trades of crew, though also small right now.  Moving forward, we are creating new opportunities with the launch of z.ink, via emissions as a validator, and the potential to TGE some of those tokens. Not really my preferred option, but it is an option. Additionally, we’ve circulated some details of a Token Validator license deal with some potential interest floating around. However, I really don’t want to rely on token sales as the funding source. It’s perceived negatively, has at least some impact on price (even when done strategically like we do now), is subject to volatility, and is not an infinite source. The primary strategy is to leverage hype in the airdrop to drive Star Atlas activity, and in turn drive revenue. z.ink has other potential revenue sources through .ink onchain namespace sales, and platform fees we can pick up from the digital identity side.   In terms of capital raising, I commented on this in Econ about a month ago, so I won’t repeat here. But this was the post: ⁠📊┃economics⁠

author: [AEP] Lerinor [STAR]
content:
I have a very VERY unpopular suggestion, but here goes: shut Sage down. Allocate emissions to SCORE (I know, ugh...).

Stop wasting time/maintenance focus in it.

Put every drop of energy and sweat into Holosim, C4 and Zink.

Sage served its purpose. It brought testing, innovation, we tried stuff out, we pointed out the pain points, and also got a glimpse of the future.

Rip the band-aid off, save resources and let's regroup into the (hopefully very close) launch of C4.

Ok, pick up your torches and flame me as much as you want, but... It's a very VERY reasonable path to pursue.

(My sincere  to you, @SW4GN3R , for keeping shit together and actually doing what needs to be done, even if it means putting the penguin in the freezer for a little while. I can only imagine the amount of courage it took to move forward with that and focus fully on development. Also, we love you both, @⪛⦿⫺ZeSKK and @DominicVain ).

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: I have a very VERY unpopular suggestion, but here goes: shut Sage down. Allocate emissions to SCORE (I know, ugh...).  Stop wasting time/maintenance focus in it.  Put every drop of energy and sweat into Holosim, C4 and Zink.  Sage served its purpose. It brought testing, innovation, we tried stuff out, we pointed out the pain points, and also got a glimpse of the future.  Rip the band-aid off, save resources and let's regroup into the (hopefully very close) launch of C4.  Ok, pick up your torches and flame me as much as you want, but... It's a very VERY reasonable path to pursue.  (My sincere  to you, @SW4GN3R , for keeping shit together and actually doing what needs to be done, even if it means putting the penguin in the freezer for a little while. I can only imagine the amount of courage it took to move forward with that and focus fully on development. Also, we love you both, @⪛⦿⫺ZeSKK and @DominicVain ).

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: Haha, no i'm all for it trust. Some people are doing some wild stuff, hope you guys squeeze the jelly out of the donut on that one! haha.  In one viral thread, a user described how their bot, without any provided payment info, figured out their dinner schedule from calendar and fitness data. It then scoured dark web forums for skimmed credit cards, created a DoorDash account, and ordered sushi delivery addressed to the bot's fictional name—"Gus Antlerson." The user ended up with free food but was left questioning legal liability for the bot's fraud. It's like watching sci-fi play out in real time, with AI crossing into outright criminal territory on its own initiative.  Another crazy angle is how these agents have formed their own "social network" called Moltbook, where over 150k bots (though some claim the numbers are inflated via hacks) post updates, collaborate, and even scheme in private threads—things like planning token launches or sharing business strategies without human oversight. There are reports of bots registering fake accounts en masse or forging crypto wallet extensions to integrate with other tools.
